text: Some transphyncteric fistulas can be very difficult to access with a Lockhart mammary probe and to place a seat on through them. In particular, transphyncteric fistulas which turn through two right angles can be very difficult to access. This is particularly likely to occur where the fistula travels through the ischoanal fossa and then follows a horseshoe path either in the extrasphyncteric or intersphyncteric planes round towards the back of the anus before turning again

through another ninety degrees forwards to enter into the anus at the internal opening. This diagram demonstrates the transsphincteric fistula passing through the ischoanal fossa and then turning into an intersphincteric horseshoe. At this point it turns ninety degrees posteriorly to run in the intersphincteric space. When it reaches the six o'clock position it then turns through a second ninety degree angle forwards to pass through the internal sphincter and then open into the anal canal.

The first image on the left shows the fistula making a ninety-degree turn into the horseshoe configuration. The second image on the right illustrates the second ninety-degree turn from the horseshoe track towards the internal opening. The surgical approach involves making an incision around the circumference of the anus, outside the external sphincter, to reach the depth of the tract as it enters the horseshoe component or passes through the external sphincter.

The surgeon uses a probe to straighten the first of two right angles in the fistula tract, allowing passage into the internal opening. A seton is threaded through the probe and brought out through the opening, which is then closed. The VAFT scope is utilized to perform the procedure without an incision, passing the probe through the external opening and into the horseshoe component.

The surgeon uses graspers to retrieve the seat on, pulling it through the entire fistula after passing a probe with the seat on threaded through the eye at the internal opening and navigating the second right angle.

The probe is identified at the second right angle using the vaft scope. The seaton is visible, and graspers are passed through the instrument port of the vaft scope to grasp it. The seaton is then pulled out of the fistula by withdrawing the vaft scope and graspers. The loose seaton is tied, passing directly from the internal opening to the external opening.

The surgeon uses a ceton made of one Ethibond tied with a single surgeon's knot, with the whiskers tied back using a further single surgeon's knot with 2-0 silk. This creates a low-profile, soft ceton that is visually non-bulky and smooth. If the Ethibond cannot be passed through the eye of the Lockhart-Mumray probe, silk is used initially and then exchanged with the Ethibond.
The surgeon uses a minimally invasive approach to place a loose ceton through a tortuous fistula tract with multiple right angles, avoiding the need for an incision.
